text: The video shows a thin, muscular male patient with minimal preperitoneal fat visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon works laterally towards Bogros space, observing that the preperitoneal plane is free of scarring and devoid of fat, allowing for easy dissection.
The surgeon works medially toward the supravesicular space, revealing the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum converging at the inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon observes a thickened hernia sac extending into the deep internal ring on the medial side.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to score the tissue, pushing the fat laterally to improve visualization. The vas deferens is not yet visible but is expected to come into view soon as the surgeon continues to inspect the area.
The surgeon performs medial dissection below Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, attempting to locate the vas deferens, which remains obscured from view.
The vas deferens begins to come into view as the surgeon continues the dissection, revealing its location amidst surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes the hernia sac, noting its edematous appearance as it is positioned near the deep internal ring on the medial side.
The surgeon uses an oblique angle to push down the spermatic vessels, attempting to improve visualization in the surgical field.

The right iliac vein becomes visible, situated close to the surrounding tissue. The surgeon approaches the area closely to ensure the tissue is properly positioned, preventing mesh buckling at this location.
The iliac vein is visible on the right side of the surgical field. The pocket appears well tailored, with high lateral fixation to prevent mesh migration.
The surgeon checks the high medial side of the mesh, ensuring no vascular structures are present on the other side.
The surgeon performs a straightforward flap closure, bringing the tissue edges together to complete the surgical repair.
